draft.introduction: Ode I.11, addressed to Leuconoe, contains Horace’s most famous phrase: carpe diem. He counsels against consulting astrologers and urges seizing the present moment, since tomorrow is uncertain and life flows away even as we speak.

translation.text: Do not ask—it is forbidden to know—what end the gods have given to me and you, Leuconoe, and do not consult Babylonian horoscopes. How much better to endure whatever shall be! Whether Jupiter has allotted many winters more, or this one now which wears out the Tyrrhenian sea against the facing rocks is the last—be wise, strain the wine, and since life is brief, cut short far-reaching hopes. Even as we speak, envious time has been fleeing. Seize the day, trusting the morrow as little as possible.
translation.original_text: Tu ne quaesieris (scire nefas) quem mihi, quem tibi finem di dederint, Leuconoe. Carpe diem, quam minimum credula postero.

translator_notes.note: Carpe diem literally means “pluck the day”—a metaphor from harvesting. Horace’s Epicurean philosophy here is practical rather than hedonistic: the point is not indulgence but the refusal to waste the present moment on anxiety about the future.
